Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}

{{AfC submission|t||ts=20220918031651|u=Immanuelle|ns=118|demo=}}

{{Expand Chinese}}

An '''Obscene temple''' is a temple{{Efn|[[:en:Ci Shrine|Ci]][[:en:Miao shrine|miao]]}} where [[Obscene sacrifice|Obscene sacrifices]] are performed<ref>[[俞正燮]]《[[癸巳类稿]]·非无鬼》：“北有野祭，南或淫祠，是天之下，地之上，皆明鬼者也。” </ref>。The [[:en:Book of Rites|Book of Rites]] condemns Obscene temples and [[Obscene sacrifice]].<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>

==Overview==

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as [[:en:Shrine|Shrine]]. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese Folk Religion]]
* [[:en:Freedom of religion|Religious Freedom]]

==References==
{{reflist}}

== See Also ==

* {{ill|Obscene sacrifice|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obscene sacrifice|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as [[:en:Shrine|Shrine]]. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese Folk Religion]]
* [[:en:Freedom of religion|Religious Freedom]]

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese Folk Religion]]
* [[:en:Freedom of religion|Religious Freedom]]

The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as [[:en:Shrine|Shrine]]. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* [[:en:Chinese folk religion|Chinese Folk Religion]]
* [[:en:Freedom of religion|Religious Freedom]]

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as {{ill|Shrine|en|Shrine|WD=Q697295|lt=Shrine}}. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Chinese folk religion|en|Chinese folk religion|WD=Q1074275|lt=Chinese Folk Religion}}

{{Expand Chinese}}

An '''Obscene temple''' is a temple{{Efn|[[:en:Ci Shrine|Ci]]{{ill|Miao shrine|en|Miao shrine|WD=Q29043583|lt=miao|1=Miao shrine}}}} where [[Obscene sacrifice|Obscene sacrifices]] are performed<ref>[[俞正燮]]《[[癸巳类稿]]·非无鬼》：“北有野祭，南或淫祠，是天之下，地之上，皆明鬼者也。” </ref>。The {{ill|Book of Rites|en|Book of Rites|WD=Q862758|lt=Book of Rites|1=Book of Rites}} condemns Obscene temples and [[Obscene sacrifice]].<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>

==Overview==

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as {{ill|Shrine|en|Shrine|WD=Q697295|lt=Shrine|1=Shrine}}. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

{{Expand Chinese}}

An '''Obscene temple''' is a temple{{Efn|[[:en:Ci Shrine|Ci]]{{ill|Miao shrine|en|Miao shrine|WD=Q29043583|lt=miao|1=Miao shrine}}}} where [[Obscene sacrifice|Obscene sacrifices]] are performed<ref>[[俞正燮]]《[[癸巳类稿]]·非无鬼》：“北有野祭，南或淫祠，是天之下，地之上，皆明鬼者也。” </ref>。The {{ill|Book of Rites|en|Book of Rites|WD=Q862758|lt=Book of Rites|1=Book of Rites}} condemns Obscene temples and [[Obscene sacrifice]].<ref>《聖祖實錄·康熙九年癸巳條》</ref>

==Overview==

'''Extraneous Shrine''' (淫祠) refers to shrines not officially sanctioned by state rituals, which are mostly self-established by the people as {{ill|Shrine|en|Shrine|WD=Q697295|lt=Shrine|1=Shrine}}. <ref>{{ill|Yu Zhengxie|de|Yu Zhengxie|ja|俞正燮|zh|俞正燮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yu Zhengxie|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’{{ill|Guisi Leigao|de|Guisi Leigao|ja|癸巳类稿|zh|癸巳类稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Guisi Leigao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Not Without Ghosts’’ states: “In the north, there are wild sacrifices; in the south, there may be extraneous shrines—beneath the heavens and above the earth, all ghosts are manifest.”</ref>.

The term “extraneous” here implies redundancy; an extraneous shrine is an unnecessary shrine, which typically results in a waste of labor and resources. The ‘’{{ill|Book of Rites|de|Das Buch der Riten|ja|禮記|zh|禮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of Rites|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 礼記|1=Book of Rites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q862758}} · Qu Li Xia’’ clearly states: “To sacrifice that which is not one’s designated deity is called extraneous worship; extraneous worship brings no fortune.” The ‘’{{ill|Song Shu|de|Song-Buch|ja|宋书|zh|宋书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Song Shu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Record of Emperor Wu, Part II’’ declares: “Extraneous shrines deceive the people and squander wealth; as mandated by earlier regulations, they should be abolished along with all such temples.”

During the Tang dynasty, acts of demolishing extraneous shrines were common. The ‘’{{ill|New Book of Tang|de|Neues Buch der Tang|ja|新唐书|zh|新唐书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Book of Tang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} · Biography of Di Renjie’’ records: “In the customs of Wu and Chu, extraneous shrines were abundant; once Di Renjie prohibited them, a total of 1,700 shrines were demolished, leaving only the four shrines dedicated to Xia Yu, Wu Taibo, Ji Za, and Wu Yuan.”

In the Song dynasty, {{ill|Chen Xiliang|de|Chen Xiliang|ja|陳希亮|zh|陳希亮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chen Xiliang|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also carried out the demolition of extraneous shrines. {{ill|Fan Zhen|de|Fan Zhen|ja|范镇|zh|范镇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fan Zhen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s ‘’Dongzhai Jishi’’, Volume 3, records: “It is further known that in Yudu County of Qianzhou, several hundred extraneous shrines were demolished, and over seventy families of shamans were compelled to register as respectable citizens.”

==See also==
* {{ill|Extraneous Worship|de|Überflüssige Verehrung|ja|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|zh|淫祀|1=Obsene sacrifice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Extraneous Worship|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Chinese folk religion|en|Chinese folk religion|WD=Q1074275|lt=Chinese Folk Religion|1=Chinese folk religion}}
* {{ill|Freedom of religion|en|Freedom of religion|WD=Q171899|lt=Religious Freedom|1=Freedom of religion}}
